title: Eros the Bittersweet
description: An essay in the form of a classical argument, Eros the Bittersweet begins with a simple observation: that desire requires distance. Carson traces the triangular structure of longing through Greek lyric poetry, Plato, and the invention of the alphabet itself. To reach toward something is already to feel its absence. Love, she argues, is not a feeling but a lack made visible. The book does not resolve this. It holds the ache open.

title: All About Love: New Visions
description: Hooks opens with a question no one seems to want to answer directly: what is love, actually. Not the feeling, not the fantasy but the practice. Drawing on M. Scott Peck's definition of love as the will to extend oneself for another's spiritual growth, she argues that most of what we call love is really need, fear, or control dressed up in softer language. The book is both a diagnosis and an instruction. It insists that love is a choice made repeatedly, in the ordinary, when it is inconvenient.

caption: Zine expressing a deeply personal message where "home" is not a place, but a person.

title: Cruel Optimism
description: A cruel optimism exists when the thing you desire is an obstacle to your own flourishing. Berlant is writing about politics and precarity, but the argument lands just as hard on love. The attachments we maintain not because they are good for us but because they organize our sense of what a life should feel like. To let go of the fantasy is to lose the structure entirely. The book sits with that difficulty without flinching. It does not ask you to stop wanting. It asks you to look clearly at what wanting costs.
